---
title: "XDL_CUDA_Programming_Guide"
subtitle: "适用于 x86_64 平台"
author: "XDL 技术支持"
date: "2026-03-17"
lang: zh-CN

format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [league, xdl-tech-theme.scss]
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        <link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.googleapis.com">
        <link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.gstatic.com" crossorigin>
        <link href="https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=IBM+Plex+Mono:wght@400;500;600&family=IBM+Plex+Sans:wght@400;500;600;700&family=Noto+Sans+SC:wght@400;500;600;700&display=swap" rel="stylesheet">
    slide-number: true
    chalkboard: false
    preview-links: auto
    small-footer: "XDL RPP"
    transition: slide
    background-transition: fade
    width: 1280
    height: 720
  pptx:
    theme: simple
    slide-number: true
    footer: "XDL RPP"
execute:
  echo: false
code-fold: false
logo: pic/logo_color_horizontal.png
reference-doc: template_xdl3.pptx
---

# XDL CUDA Programming Guide (RPP)

This guide explains how to write RPP CUDA-style programs on XDL, based on these demos:

- `rpp_first_demo`
- `rpp_manual_sram`
- `rpp_pingpong`
- `rpp_tile_based`

The goal is to help customers go from "first working kernel" to "pipeline-ready async/tile design."



## 1) What Is Different on RPP

RPP is an accelerator with a memory model that is similar to CUDA concepts, but with explicit SRAM usage patterns:

- Host memory (CPU): `malloc` / `free`
- Device DDR (global memory on card): `rtMalloc` / `rtFree`
- On-chip SRAM (fast local memory): managed by:
  - `rppsmgr::SRamManager` (`smgr.Download`, `smgr.Upload`) or
  - manual SRAM APIs (`rtMallocSram`, `rtMemcpyDeviceToSram`, `rtMemcpySramToDevice`)

Core data path in most programs:

```text
Host -> Device DDR -> SRAM -> Kernel -> SRAM -> Device DDR -> Host
```

If customers understand this path, they understand the foundation of RPP programming.



## 2) Learning Path from the Demos

### Demo 1: `rpp_first_demo` (SRamManager, easiest start)

Use this when teaching first-time users:

- Keep kernel simple (`C = A + B`)
- Allocate DDR with `rtMalloc`
- Register buffers with `smgr.PitchAllocate`
- Move data with `smgr.Download` / `smgr.Upload`
- Launch kernel with SRAM pointers from `smgr.GetSRamAddr`

Best for: first successful program and first memory-flow understanding.

### Demo 2: `rpp_manual_sram` (explicit SRAM control)

Use this when customers need precise memory control:

- Allocate SRAM buffers with `rtMallocSram`
- Move data using explicit directions:
  - DDR -> SRAM: `rtMemcpy(..., rtMemcpyDeviceToSram)`
  - SRAM -> DDR: `rtMemcpy(..., rtMemcpySramToDevice)`

Best for: understanding exact data movement and debugging memory pipelines.

### Demo 3: `rpp_pingpong` (async streams + events)

Use this when customers want overlap-ready pipeline structure:

- Two streams:
  - transfer stream for memory copies
  - compute stream for kernels
- Event dependencies:
  - `rppEventRecord`
  - `rppStreamWaitEvent`
- Ping-pong SRAM buffers to avoid read/write hazards

Best for: throughput-oriented design and cold/warm run analysis.

### Demo 4: `rpp_tile_based` (tile pipeline)

Use this when matrices are large and customers need chunked processing:

- Split matrix into tiles
- Alternate slot0/slot1 in SRAM
- For each tile: preload -> compute -> store
- Preload next tile while current tile is computing/storing

Best for: scalable memory-efficient pipeline design.



## 3) Standard Program Skeleton

Use this as a template for new customer programs:

```text
1. Define shape and bytes (width/height/pitch/bytes)
2. Allocate host buffers and initialize inputs
3. Allocate device DDR buffers (rtMalloc)
4. Allocate/register SRAM buffers
5. Create streams/events if async pipeline is needed
6. Copy H2D
7. Copy DDR->SRAM (or smgr.Download)
8. Launch kernel on SRAM pointers
9. Copy SRAM->DDR (or smgr.Upload)
10. Copy D2H
11. Validate outputs and print key metrics
12. Destroy/free all resources
```



## 4) API Cheat Sheet by Stage

### Allocation

- Host: `malloc`, `free`
- Device DDR: `rtMalloc`, `rtFree`
- SRAM manual: `rtMallocSram`, `rtFreeSram`
- SRAM managed: `smgr.Init`, `smgr.PitchAllocate`, `smgr.Destroy`

### Data Movement

- H2D: `rtMemcpy(..., rtMemcpyHostToDevice)` or `rppMemcpyHtoDAsync`
- DDR->SRAM:
  - managed: `smgr.Download`
  - manual: `rtMemcpy(..., rtMemcpyDeviceToSram)` or `rppMemcpyDtoSAsync`
- SRAM->DDR:
  - managed: `smgr.Upload`
  - manual: `rtMemcpy(..., rtMemcpySramToDevice)` or `rppMemcpyStoDAsync`
- D2H: `rtMemcpy(..., rtMemcpyDeviceToHost)` or `rppMemcpyDtoHAsync`

### Synchronization

- Full device sync: `rtDeviceSynchronize`
- Stream sync: `rppStreamSynchronize`
- Event flow control: `rppEventRecord`, `rppStreamWaitEvent`



## 5) Design Decision Guide

Use this quick rule for customers:

- First project / training:
  - Start with `rpp_first_demo` style (SRamManager)
- Need strict memory control:
  - Use `rpp_manual_sram` style
- Need async execution / overlap-ready pipeline:
  - Use `rpp_pingpong` style
- Need large-scale chunked processing:
  - Use `rpp_tile_based` style



## 6) Common Mistakes to Avoid

- Forgetting DDR<->SRAM transfer before/after kernel.
- Mixing transfer direction flags (`DeviceToSram` vs `SramToDevice`).
- Launching compute before load completion when using async streams.
- Not waiting on events in ping-pong pipeline.
- Freeing stream/event/SRAM resources too early.
- Tile configuration mismatch (e.g., matrix height not aligned to tile height).



## 7) Validation Strategy for Customers

For every new kernel, keep validation simple and consistent:

1. Print first N values (`A`, `B`, `C`) to check math quickly.
2. Print launch configuration (`threads`, `blocks`).
3. Print stage timing:
   - H2D
   - DDR->SRAM
   - Kernel
   - SRAM->DDR
   - D2H
4. Compare cold run and warm run when async pipeline is used.



## 8) Build and Run Pattern

Each demo follows the same command pattern:

```bash
mkdir -p build
cd build
cmake ..
make -j
./<demo_binary_name>
```

Examples:

- `./rpp_first_demo`
- `./rpp_manual_sram`
- `./rpp_pingpong`
- `./rpp_tile_based`



## 9) Recommended Customer Training Sequence

Use this order in onboarding sessions:

1. `rpp_first_demo`: run and explain full memory path.
2. `rpp_manual_sram`: remove abstraction and show explicit transfer directions.
3. `rpp_pingpong`: introduce stream/event dependency model.
4. `rpp_tile_based`: teach practical scaling strategy for real workloads.

By the end of step 4, customers can build production-style RPP pipelines with clear memory ownership and execution dependency control.
